title: -Update on Implementation Progress of Citygate’s Workload Organizational Study of the Police Department

sections.background:
The Citygate Workload and Organizational Study, delivered in September 2024,
presented a menu of 54 recommendations and 74 key findings stemming from an in-
depth analysis of the department's workload, staffing, organizational structure, and
overtime management. The Citygate report has been instrumental in helping the
Berkeley Police Department (BPD) set strategic priorities and continues to serve as a
valuable guide for the department's ongoing efforts to enhance efficiency, optimize
resource deployment, and improve service delivery to the Berkeley community. These
recommendations are meant to be used as a suite of options that will inform our
operational and strategic decisions over the long term, rather than a prescriptive
checklist for immediate full completion. This approach allows the department to remain
fiscally responsible and strategically adaptive, particularly in light of the City's structural
deficit.
To provide context on the scope of these recommendations and our approach:
 14 recommendations are either already completed (such as the Bike Team
realignment detailed below) or represent ongoing departmental work that aligned
with Citygate's findings (e.g., continuous recruitment efforts, ongoing data
analysis).
 18 of the recommendations call for the creation of new staff positions.
 12 additional recommendations, while not requiring new positions, would
necessitate new funding for implementation (e.g., for technology, equipment, or
specific programs).
 The remaining 10 recommendations have been identified as low priority, would
require significant existing staff time to implement (which is currently
constrained), or involve potential long-term labor negotiation considerations.

Outlined below are significant achievements that have been implemented to
sections.current_situation:
BPD has made significant progress, with 14 of the 54 recommendations now complete
or representing ongoing work that was already aligned with Citygate's findings. These
achievements have been accomplished through the strategic reallocation of existing
resources.
Select In-Progress & Completed Initiatives:
 Use of Force Reporting Policy Review
o BPD has a comprehensive review and update of our Use of Force
reporting policy well underway and hopes to launch new edits soon. This
is a critical area highlighted by Citygate, which suggested aligning our
reporting thresholds for certain lower-level force incidents with common
practices in comparable agencies, while ensuring full compliance with
state and federal law and maintaining robust accountability. The
Department is committed to a transparent process and will provide the
Police Accountability Board (PAB) with advance notice before the
implementation date so that their input can be considered on version edits.
 Realigning Resources for Enhanced Community Engagement
o Consistent with Citygate's analysis on optimizing divisional structures, the
Bike Team has been realigned under the Community Services Bureau
(CSB). This move is intended to increase CSB's capacity to address
community concerns and improve operational oversight within that bureau.
 Implementing Alternative Response Models – Community Service Officers
(CSOs)
o The Patrol CSO program is now operational, a key step towards
addressing varied calls for service more efficiently. The Department is
pleased to report that three Community Service Officers have successfully
completed their comprehensive training program. These CSOs are now
independently responding to appropriate low-risk calls for service in the
field, such as taking reports for non-emergency incidents, recovering
stolen vehicles and found property, and assisting with traffic control. This
initiative directly supports Citygate’s recommendation to utilize CSOs to
handle tasks not requiring a sworn
